text: Chapter five of the Enchiridion addresses the distress people feel at a relative's death or illness. Epictetus does not deny the grief; he examines what drives it past its natural limit. It is not the death itself but our judgment that the death is terrible — a judgment we install — that becomes the source of prolonged suffering. He asks: does the hardship come from outside, or from your interpretation? This is not a trick to suppress emotion. It is an invitation to examine the invisible layer of commentary we add to every event. That commentary, unlike the event, is genuinely ours to revise.
question: According to Epictetus in chapter five of the Enchiridion, what is the actual source of prolonged suffering around a death?

options.text: The physical absence of the person who has died
Insufficient mourning rituals in Roman society
The failure to accept mortality as a philosophical principle
The judgment that the death is terrible — a commentary we add to the event
